description: Weather conditions, nature, and outdoor environment

items.notes: A very common word. The long vowel 'ää' is important — pronounce it clearly.
examples: Millainen sää tänään on? — What's the weather like today?

items.notes: Stem: aurinko → auringon (genitive). The 'nk' changes to 'ng' in inflected forms.
examples: Aurinko paistaa. — The sun is shining.

items.notes: Pilvinen means 'cloudy'. Stem stays regular: pilve-.
examples: Taivaalla on paljon pilviä. — There are many clouds in the sky.

items.notes: Stem changes: sade → satee- (e.g., sateen, sadetta). The verb 'to rain' is 'sataa'.
examples: Huomenna tulee sadetta. — Tomorrow there will be rain.

items.notes: Stem: lumi → lume- (e.g., lumen, lunta). Finland gets snow from November to April.
examples: Ulkona sataa lunta. — It's snowing outside.

items.notes: Stem: tuuli → tuule- (e.g., tuulen, tuulta). Tuulinen = windy.
examples: Tänään on kova tuuli. — Today there is a strong wind.

items.notes: Stem: myrsky → myrskyä (partitive). Finnish storms are common over the Baltic Sea in autumn.
examples: Myrsky on tulossa. — A storm is coming.

items.notes: Stem: ukkonen → ukkose- (e.g., ukkosen, ukkosta). Related to 'Ukko', the Finnish thunder god in mythology.
examples: Ukkonen jyrisee. — Thunder is rumbling.

items.notes: Stem stays regular: salama-. 'Salamoida' is the verb meaning 'to flash with lightning'.
examples: Salama iski puuhun. — Lightning struck a tree.

items.notes: Stem stays regular: sumu-. 'Sumuinen' means 'foggy'. Common near Finnish lakes in autumn mornings.
examples: Aamulla oli tiheä sumu. — In the morning there was thick fog.

items.notes: Long vowel 'ää'. Jäätelö = ice cream, jääkaappi = refrigerator (literally 'ice cabinet').
examples: Järven jää on paksu. — The lake ice is thick.

items.notes: Stem: lämmin → lämpimä- (e.g., lämpimän, lämmintä). Note the stem change.
examples: Tänään on lämmin päivä. — Today is a warm day.

items.notes: One of the most useful Finnish words! Minulla on kylmä = I am cold (literally 'I have cold').
examples: Ulkona on kylmä. — It's cold outside.

items.notes: Used for weather and temperature. Kuuma vesi = hot water. Sauna on kuuma!
examples: Kesällä on kuuma. — In summer it's hot.

items.notes: Between 'kylmä' (cold) and 'lämmin' (warm). Very common in Finnish weather — many days are 'viileä'.
examples: Ilta on viileä. — The evening is cool.

items.notes: Stem: pakkanen → pakkase- (e.g., pakkasen, pakkasta). Refers to sub-zero temperatures. A very Finnish concept — winter temperatures can reach -30°C or colder.
examples: Tänään on kova pakkanen. — Today there is a hard frost.

items.notes: Finland is about 73% forest — the most forested country in Europe.
examples: Menen metsään kävelylle. — I'm going to the forest for a walk.

items.notes: Stem: järvi → järve- (e.g., järven, järveä). Finland has approximately 188,000 lakes — the 'Land of a Thousand Lakes'!
examples: Suomessa on paljon järviä. — In Finland there are many lakes.

items.notes: Stem: joki → joe- (e.g., joen, jokea). Many Finnish city names contain 'joki'.
examples: Joki virtaa metsän läpi. — The river flows through the forest.

items.notes: Stem: meri → mere- (e.g., meren, merta). Finland borders the Baltic Sea.
examples: Meri on kaunis kesällä. — The sea is beautiful in summer.

items.notes: Stem: saari → saare- (e.g., saaren, saarta). Finland has over 170,000 islands in its archipelago.
examples: Asumme pienellä saarella. — We live on a small island.

items.notes: Stem: vuori → vuore- (e.g., vuoren, vuorta). Finland is mostly flat — 'tunturi' (fell) is more common for Finnish highlands in Lapland.
examples: Vuori on korkea. — The mountain is high.

items.notes: Stem: taivas → taivaa- (e.g., taivaan, taivasta). Also means 'heaven'. The stem change drops the 's'.
examples: Taivas on sininen. — The sky is blue.

items.notes: Stem: tähti → tähde- (e.g., tähden, tähteä). Note the 'ti' → 'de' consonant gradation in inflected forms.
examples: Tähdet loistavat taivaalla. — The stars shine in the sky.

items.notes: Has two meanings: 'moon' and 'month'. Context makes it clear. Tammikuu = January (literally 'oak month').
examples: Kuu loistaa kirkkaasti. — The moon shines brightly.

items.notes: Stem: kukka → kuka- (e.g., kukan, kukkaa). The double 'kk' reduces to single 'k' in some forms.
examples: Puutarhassa kasvaa kauniita kukkia. — Beautiful flowers grow in the garden.

items.notes: Means both 'tree' and 'wood' (the material). Puinen = wooden. Puutarha = garden (literally 'tree yard').
examples: Puu kasvaa puutarhassa. — A tree grows in the garden.
